content:
# 1 Timothy Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TI_005_page_001.jpg

1. Do not rebuke an older man but encourage him as you would a father, younger men as brothers,

2. older women as mothers, younger women as sisters, in all purity.

3. Honor widows who are truly widows.

4. But if a widow has children or grandchildren, let them first learn to show godliness to their own household and to make some return to their parents, for this is pleasing in the sight of God.

5. She who is truly a widow, left all alone, has set her hope on God and continues in supplications and prayers night and day,

6. but she who is self-indulgent is dead even while she lives.

7. Command these things as well, so that they may be without reproach.

8. But if anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.

9. Let a widow be enrolled if she is not less than sixty years of age, having been the wife of one husband,

10. and having a reputation for good works: if she has brought up children, has shown hospitality, has washed the feet of the saints, has cared for the afflicted, and has devoted herself to every good work.

11. But refuse to enroll younger widows, for when their passions draw them away from Christ, they desire to marry

12. and so incur condemnation for having abandoned their former pledge.

13. Besides that, they learn to be idlers, going about from house to house, and not only idlers but also gossips and busybodies, saying what they should not.

14. So I would have younger widows marry, bear children, manage their households, and give the adversary no occasion for slander.

15. For some have already strayed after Satan.

16. If any believing woman has relatives who are widows, let her care for them. Let the church not be burdened, so that it may care for those who are truly widows.

17. Let the elders who rule well be considered worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in preaching and teaching.

18. For the Scripture says, "You shall not muzzle an ox when it treads out the grain," and, "The laborer deserves his wages."

19. Do not admit a charge against an elder except on the evidence of two or three witnesses.

20. As for those who persist in sin, rebuke them in the presence of all, so that the rest may stand in fear.

21. In the presence of God and of Christ Jesus and of the elect angels I charge you to keep these rules without prejudging, doing nothing from partiality.

22. Do not be hasty in the laying on of hands, nor take part in the sins of others. Keep yourself pure.

23. (No longer drink only water, but use a little wine for the sake of your stomach and your frequent ailments.)

24. The sins of some people are conspicuous, going before them to judgment, but the sins of others appear later.

25. So also good works are conspicuous, and even those that are not cannot remain hidden.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 5 addresses pastoral relationships (vv.1-2), the widow order (vv.3-16), elder oversight (vv.17-25), and includes a personal health note (v.23). The widow regulations are the most detailed institutional passage in the Pastorals.

**Verse 18 — Double citation as "Scripture":** All three translators preserved both quotations. SOPHIA affirms: the citation of a Jesus tradition (parallel to Luke 10:7) as "Scripture" alongside Deuteronomy is significant evidence for the early authority of Gospel traditions.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.3 — "Honor widows who are truly widows" vs. "Take care of any widow who has no one else to care for her"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Honor widows, the [ones] truly [being] widows" | chēras tima tas ontōs chēras |
| LOGOS | "Honor widows who are truly widows" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Take care of any widow who has no one else to care for her" | "Take care of" for "honor"; adds "who has no one else to care for her" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says "honor" (tima) widows who are "truly" (ontōs) widows. PNEUMA changes "honor" to "take care of" (anticipating the financial support context) and adds "who has no one else to care for her" — a correct inference from vv.4-5 but not in this verse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA anticipates context and adds.

---

### v.11 — "drawn by desire against the Christos" vs. "their passions draw them away from Christ" vs. "their physical desires will overpower their devotion to Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "when they are drawn by desire against the Christos, they wish to marry" | hotan gar katasthrēniasōsin tou Christou |
| LOGOS | "when their passions draw them away from Christ, they desire to marry" | "Passions draw them away" |
| PNEUMA | "their physical desires will overpower their devotion to Christ and they will want to remarry" | "Physical desires"; "overpower their devotion"; "remarry" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. katasthrēniaō means "to feel sensual impulses against" — the desires work against their commitment to Christ. PNEUMA adds "physical" (specifying the type of desire), "overpower their devotion" (dramatizes), and changes "marry" to "remarry" (interpretive — they may not have been previously married).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "physical," dramatizes, and changes "marry" to "remarry."

---

### v.17 — "double honor" vs. "respected and paid well"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "considered worthy of double honor" | diplēs timēs |
| LOGOS | "considered worthy of double honor" | Same — preserves dual sense |
| PNEUMA | "should be respected and paid well" | Splits timē into two: "respected" + "paid well" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. timē means both "honor/respect" and "payment/price." Paul's "double honor" deliberately holds both senses together. PNEUMA splits into "respected and paid well" — making explicit what Paul leaves combined.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA splits the dual-sense term.

---

### v.21 — "the chosen angels" vs. "the elect angels" vs. "the highest angels"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the chosen angels" | tōn eklektōn angelōn |
| LOGOS | "the elect angels" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the highest angels" | "Highest" — changes "elect" to "highest" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. eklektos means "chosen/elect" — not "highest." PNEUMA changes to "highest," introducing a hierarchy concept where Paul uses an election concept. The "elect angels" are the angels who remained faithful (as opposed to fallen angels).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes "elect" to "highest."

---

### v.22 — "Lay hands hastily on no one" vs. "Never be in a hurry about appointing a church leader"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lay hands hastily on no one" | cheiras tacheōs mēdeni epitithei |
| LOGOS | "Do not be hasty in the laying on of hands" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Never be in a hurry about appointing a church leader" | "Appointing a church leader" — interprets; drops "laying on of hands" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Laying on of hands" is a specific ritual act — ordination/commissioning. PNEUMA replaces with "appointing a church leader," losing the ritual act and specifying "church leader" where Paul leaves the role unspecified.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA drops ritual act and specifies role.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 2 | "Who has no one" (v.3), "physical" / "remarry" (v.11) |
| PNEUMA splits dual-sense terms | 1 | "Double honor" → "respected and paid well" (v.17) |
| PNEUMA changes terms | 2 | "Elect" → "highest" (v.21), "laying on of hands" → "appointing" (v.22) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 5's most notable disagreements involve PNEUMA splitting or changing terms that carry dual meanings. "Double honor" (timē = respect + payment) is split into two separate concepts, and the ritual act of "laying on of hands" is replaced with the abstract "appointing." Paul's language deliberately holds multiple meanings together; PNEUMA separates them.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
